ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for beancurd seafood claypot. FOODON_03302051 (tofu-based dish) is the closest defensible parent. Seafood and claypot cooking method are not captured at this level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers cooked tofu/soybean products. The seafood component and claypot preparation are not individually represented; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific mixed seafood-tofu claypot dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of beancurd seafood in claypot is approximately 350 g, representing a shared portion for one to two persons as part of a multi-dish Chinese meal.

nutrient_source.title: Tofu with Seafood, braised — adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exactly matches 'Beancurd Seafood in Claypot'. Values are estimated by compositing HPB entries for braised tofu dishes (e.g., 'Tofu with Seafood') and USDA FoodData Central data for mixed seafood components. The dish is approximately 40% firm tofu, 35% mixed seafood (prawns, squid, fish), 15% sauce/stock, and 10% aromatics/vegetables. Energy and macros are weighted accordingly. Sodium is elevated due to oyster sauce and soy sauce seasoning typical of this dish style.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soybean curd (tofu) and mixed seafood dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tofu and low-GI seafood-based dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beancurd seafood in claypot. GI estimated at 32 based on: (1) University of Sydney GI Database reports tofu/soybean curd at GI ~15 (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); (2) mixed seafood dishes with minimal starch have GI in the 25–40 range; (3) the small amount of cornstarch thickener and oyster sauce contribute modest glycemic load. Weighted composite GI of approximately 32 is defensible. GL per 350 g serving = (32 × 4.5 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 5.0 g. gi_category is 'low' per ≤55 threshold.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a 350 g serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium — nearly 64% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by high protein and soy-based tofu content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beancurd seafood in claypot has a low estimated GI of approximately 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 5 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals managing blood glucose, provided sodium-heavy sauces are moderated.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from seafood, offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and soy isoflavones
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides beneficial marine omega-3 fatty acids and soy isoflavones from tofu, which support cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content from seasoning sauces warrants caution for individuals with established cardiovascular disease or salt-sensitive hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beancurd Seafood in Claypot (砂锅海鲜豆腐) is a popular Chinese-style braised dish served in a traditional clay or ceramic pot that retains heat and imparts a subtle earthy flavour. The dish combines firm tofu (beancurd) with a medley of fresh seafood — typically prawns, squid, and fish fillet, and in premium restaurant versions, crab or lobster — braised together in a savoury sauce made from oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and seafood or chicken stock, lightly thickened with cornstarch. The claypot cooking method allows the tofu to absorb the umami-rich seafood broth, resulting in a silky, flavourful texture. The dish is a staple of Singaporean Chinese seafood restaurants and zi char (煮炒) stalls, typically ordered as a shared dish alongside steamed rice. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with a low glycemic index, making it suitable for blood glucose management. The tofu contributes calcium, soy isoflavones, and plant protein, while the seafood provides lean animal protein, vitamin B12, selenium, and marine omega-3 fatty acids. Sodium content is elevated due to the seasoning sauces, which is a primary concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free but contains soy, shellfish, crustacean, fish, and gluten allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry for beancurd seafood in claypot exists. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing HPB braised tofu dish data and USDA FoodData Central seafood component data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for tofu and low-GI seafood dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high quality and the dish composition is well-defined.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central composite sources used for this estimation; the value cannot be defensibly quantified from available approved sources and is therefore recorded as missing per the sentinel value convention. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0 as this dish uses vegetable oil and seafood with no industrially hydrogenated fats; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central component data. Monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 not applicable here; value provided as 1.8), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.7) are estimated from USDA component data. Nutrient values represent a typical restaurant preparation; home-cooked versions may vary significantly in sodium and fat content depending on sauce quantities. The dish is not halal-certified as a category because it may contain non-halal seafood preparations or cross-contamination in restaurant settings; individual restaurant halal status should be verified. GI is estimated at 32 (low category, ≤55 threshold) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for tofu and mixed seafood dishes.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record with composite nutrient estimation from HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central. Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual names. Set GI to 32 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for tofu and seafood dishes.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Verified trans_fat_g 0.0 justified. Verified GI category 'low' consistent with GI=32 ≤55 threshold. Confirmed all hard gates pass and score 8/8.

Iteration 3: Fixed g3_numeric_hygiene failure. Expanded notes field to explicitly document vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel value with full justification: 'Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central composite sources used for this estimation; the value cannot be defensibly quantified from available approved sources and is therefore recorded as missing per the sentinel value convention.' Simultaneously corrected Hindi string in local_names, aliases, and aliases_by_language to use consistent standard Devanagari spelling 'समुद्री' ensuring exact verbatim match across all three locations.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
